This underground network was carved into volcanic rock over 2,000 years ago

The tunnels were first created by the ancient Greeks, later expanded by the Romans into aqueducts, and eventually used as bomb shelters during WWII where thousands of people hid and survived underground for months.

Walking through it honestly feels unreal.

There are massive hidden chambers, ancient tunnels, underground water systems, passageways, and even extremely narrow sections where you have to squeeze sideways through the walls

This is…

📍Napoli Sotterranea, Naples, Italy

Tour time is around 1.5–2 hours
Wear comfortable shoes because parts can be slippery and tight
You have to go down and climb up a lot of stairs

caption:

🤍 Follow for more unique places and roadtrips around the world @tripuntraveled

Most people have heard of Paris but may not have heard of Paris Syndrome and I learned about it recently…but it’s a real phenomenon.

The term became famous after some Japanese tourists experienced severe culture shock when visiting Paris.

Many were expecting the city they had seen in movies, advertisements, and magazines with elegant streets, fashionable locals, romance around every corner, and a picture perfect atmosphere.

Instead, they found -
traffic
crowds
graffiti
pickpockets
cigarette smell
and people trying to get to work

For some visitors, the gap between expectation and reality became so overwhelming that they experienced symptoms like anxiety, panic, dizziness, and extreme distress.

Psychologists eventually gave the phenomenon a name and called it Paris Syndrome.

The funny thing is that Paris isn’t the problem. The problem is expecting a real city to live up to a fantasy.

Have you experienced Paris Syndrome or did you fall in love with the city?

At first glance, this place looks like a cathedral or palace…but it was actually built for silk merchants and businessmen.

The main trading hall is one of the most beautiful interiors I’ve seen in Spain with giant spiraling columns stretching toward the ceiling like palm trees or vines.

The architecture was intentionally designed to impress, intimidate, and show off the city’s wealth to foreign traders arriving here in the 1400s.

and there was even a prison built into the complex for merchants who couldn’t pay their debts

This is

📍La Lonja de la Seda, Valencia, Spain

Things to know
it was built in the late 15th century during Valencia’s silk boom
it’s a UNESCO World Heritage Site
it’s one of the greatest examples of Gothic civil architecture in Europe

caption:
I never grew up thinking, “One day, I’ll be a content creator.” 

👩‍💻I was supposed to follow the safe path.
I went to school, got my degree, landed my 9-5 job in the advanced air mobility industry and I’m VERY proud of that. I get to be part of very few people shaping the future of air mobility. 

But deep down… I always felt this quiet pull towards something more. I always find myself chasing little pockets of the world that most people skip or haven’t even heard of! The places not trending on Instagram and tiny villages where you get lost on purpose.

So I picked up my camera 📸 and started sharing stories through not just places, but emotions. Slowly, my small corner of Instagram grew into a beautiful community of people who are always looking for something unique or different.

✅ I want to be a content creator to inspire 
people who feel stuck, 
people who think travel isn’t for them,
people who think they need to quit their jobs to see the world. 
You don’t. I didn’t.
✅ You can chase your dreams without sacrificing stability.
✅ You can start before you’re “ready.”
‼️ You can live fully even if the world tells you to choose one path.

It’s possible to build a life where one day, I can turn this into something even bigger ✈️ my own business, my own brand, my own freedom. Remember, “you don’t need to be great to start but you NEED TO start in order to be great”. 

and if one person watches my content and thinks “Wait… maybe I can do this too”..that’s the real win

This medieval villa was once one of the largest and most expensive estates on the entire Amalfi Coast.

It was built in the 1200s by the wealthy Rufolo family, and was designed to show power, status, and wealth at a time when Ravello was an important trading town connected to the Mediterranean world.

Today, parts of the villa are still in ruins. You walk through Moorish arches, stone towers, hidden courtyards, and gardens hanging above the sea.

This is…

📍 Villa Rufolo, Ravello, Amalfi Coast, Italy

🚗 How to get here
The easiest way is by car or private driver from Amalfi, Positano, or Salerno. Ravello sits high above the coastline so expect winding mountain roads with incredible views the entire way.

🎟️ Things to know
The villa is famous for its cliffside gardens and terrace overlooking the Amalfi Coast. If you visit in summer, Ravello festival concerts are sometimes held here

caption:
‼️Venice - overrated or unforgettable?👇🏻

🤍 Follow for unique places and roadtrips around the world @tripuntraveled

I finally understand why people either LOVE Venice or absolutely hate it.

But I have to say, those who hate it might be doing this -
they arrive midday
fight crowds around Rialto and San Marco
eat at tourist restaurants
leave a few hours later
and then wonder what the hype was about 🤔

But the biggest Venice hack is to STAY OVERNIGHT

Where to stay?
The San Marco / Grand Canal area for classic luxury Venice and iconic views
Cannaregio for quieter canals and local feel
Dorsoduro for artsy, romantic Venice

Most underrated experience?
Taking a private taxi down the Grand Canal at sunset

For a special experience?
Book one nice aperitivo or dinner with canal views instead of standing in lines. Atmosphere matters in Venice

Beautiful stays worth looking into
@stregisvenice
@amanvenice
@thegrittipalace
@belmondhotelcipriani

What surprised me the most?
Venice doesn’t feel impressive because of one landmark. It feels impressive because an entire city lives on water.

Then there are tiny bookstores, hidden courtyards, quiet canals, faded buildings, laundry hanging above the water…it feels more like a film set than a city sometimes.

📍 Venice, Italy

📌 Save this for your Venice trip

So…Overrated or unforgettable?

caption:
‼️The Tuscany road trip you will think about forever👇🏻

🤍 Follow for more unique places and road trips around the world @tripuntraveled

This is the route I would actually do if you want medieval towers, hilltop towns, wine villages, cypress roads, Renaissance streets, and countryside views.

📍Volterra
Start here if you want Tuscany with an ancient, mysterious feeling. This town has Etruscan roots, a Roman theatre, medieval streets, and alabaster shops that make it feel completely different

📍San Gimignano
This is the famous medieval Manhattan of Tuscany, with 14 surviving towers still rising above the town. Go early or later in the day because this one gets busy, but the skyline is absolutely worth seeing once in your life

📍Certaldo Alto
This is one of the most underrated stops on this route. It is small, brick colored, quiet, and tied to Giovanni Boccaccio, the author of The Decameron. Take the funicular up

📍Montalcino
Come here for Brunello wine and fortress views. This is a good stop for wine tasting or a long countryside meal.

📍San Quirico d’Orcia
Beautiful and unreal close to the famous cypress trees, Chapel of Madonna di Vitaleta, Horti Leonini, and some of the most iconic Val d’Orcia landscapes.

📍Pienza
End here for the soft, golden, romantic version of Tuscany. Pienza was redesigned as an ideal Renaissance town, and will make you fall in love with Tuscany.

🚗 How to plan this route
Do not try to squeeze all of this into one rushed day unless you enjoy suffering beautifully.

Day 1 - Volterra, San Gimignano, Certaldo
Day 2 - Montalcino, San Quirico d’Orcia, Pienza

But if you have more time, make it 3 days and stay overnight in the countryside.

📌 Save this for your Tuscany trip because this route gives you the best mix of medieval towns, wine country, cypress views, and more.

caption:
‼️if you are visiting Venice this year👇🏻 

🤍 Follow for more unique places and travel guides around the world

Getting there

Fly into Venice Marco Polo Airport. From there, you can take a water taxi (expensive but iconic) or the Alilaguna water bus (budget-friendly). 

If you’re coming by train, Venezia Santa Lucia drops you straight into the heart of the city.

Where to stay

If it’s your first time, stay near San Marco or along the Grand Canal so you can walk everywhere early in the morning before crowds take over.

🤍 I stayed at the @stregisvenice and it was an epic experience. One of my favorite moments of staying here was that every evening they carry on a ritual called champagne sabering - where a bottle is opened with a sword (yes, an actual sword) and I actually got to do it myself and then try it 🤍

Things to do

Get lost. Not even kidding! Venice is best experienced with no plan
Wake up early and walk through Piazza San Marco before 8 AM
Take a vaporetto or gondola ride down the Grand Canal
Visit Burano for colorful houses or Murano for glassmaking
Climb the Campanile for one of the best views in the city

What to eat

Skip the tourist places right by the main squares.
Walk a few streets away and look for smaller, local spots.
Try cicchetti (Venetian tapas), fresh pasta, and tiramisu (this is where it was born).
Also, don’t leave without having a spritz by the water.

Things no one tells you ⚠️

Venice is beautiful, but it’s also crowded. Early mornings and late evenings are your best friend
Restaurants near major attractions are often overpriced and not worth it
Pack light! There are a LOT of bridges and no Ubers here
Acqua alta (flooding) can happen, especially in certain months, so check ahead

they are NOT even close!

One is slow, colorful, cozy, and easy and the other is dramatic, luxurious, and cinematic.

And I think choosing the wrong one for your travel style and in the wrong season is exactly why some people leave disappointed.

I have broken down all the categories like landscapes, transportation, costs, architecture, and more to help you decide between the two if you can only visit one.

I personally loved both for completely different reasons.

Also…I strongly suggest visiting the Amalfi Coast in winter. It was one of the best Italy experiences I’ve ever had. There were no crowds or traffic, and the entire coastline felt surreal (I had it all to myself).

There were no crowds in Cinque Terre either which made exploring a breeze.

Apparently when Japanese people started traveling to Europe in the second half of the 20th century and, after hearing wonders about Paris and picturing the city of love and lights, found themselves in a city covered in dirt and garbage and full of rats, thieves and unfriendly locals, they got so depressed and disillusioned that after going home they were diagnosed with "Paris Syndrome". Symptoms include extreme anxiety, feeling of persecution, profound disappointment and homesickness, dizziness, nausea, insomnia, rapid heartbeat, hallucinations and delusions.

caption:

🤍 Follow for more unique places and roadtrips around the world @tripuntraveled

Most people visit this place for the Renaissance streets, sunset views, and famous piazza from Twilight saga’s New Moon

but underneath the town, there is an entire hidden underground world that most people have no idea exists

This is the Underground City inside Ercolani Cellars where ancient tunnels, hidden chambers, passageways, wells, and historic spaces stretch beneath the streets.

Some parts date back centuries and it genuinely feels like discovering a secret

Honestly, this ended up being one of the coolest experiences I had in Tuscany

This is…

📍 Ercolani Cellars Underground City
Montepulciano, Tuscany, Italy

🚗 How to get here
Montepulciano is best visited by car as part of a Tuscany road trip through Val d’Orcia. Ercolani Cellars is located directly inside the historic center.

What to do
walk through underground tunnels and chambers beneath Montepulciano while learning about the hidden history underneath the town. There are ancient artifacts, Etruscan elements, historic wells, and cellar spaces throughout the experience. 

⚠️ There are free guided tours with wine tasting

📌 Save this for your Tuscany trip

caption:
If you are planning to visit Utah and are impacted by the new National Park entry rules, these must visit spots outside the National Parks are the perfect way to experience Utah 🫶😍

✨Hi! We’re Palak and Vaibhav, a couple based in eastern USA who loves to travel and enjoy the beautiful outdoors. Follow along our journey if you like to hike, go on roadtrips and travel the world with your partner ♥️

🪨 Factory Butte- This otherworldly monolith is 6,300 ft or 1,920 m high and you can drive very close to it with a bit of off roading
🚘 Potash Road- Stunning drive going through a beautiful red rock canyon with massive red rock wall on one side and the Colorado River on the other
🌈 Bentonite Hills- A stunning display of colors makes this spot really special. It is nearby NASA’s Mars Desert Research Station in Hanksville
🌘 Moon Overlook- Also called Moonscape Overlook or Skyline Rim Overlook, this spot will really make you think you’re on the moon
🛻 Marlboro Point- This amazing hidden gem is accessible via a 4x4 if you want to make it all the way to the point. You can opt to park around 0.4 mile away if you are more comfortable with that
🏜️ Goblin Valley State Park- Dense hoodoos define the surreal landscape of this beautiful park. A $20 fee is required for this state park
🗼 Long Dong Silver- A strikingly beautiful Spire in Hanksville, Utah. Requires ~1 mile hike to reach this spot
🐎 Dead Horse Point State Park- One of the best sunrise we have ever seen was inside this park. The warm light of the sunrise illuminates the red rocks with a striking red glow. We highly recommend a visit here

caption:
This fairytale town has heart shaped cutouts on the windows…and centuries ago, they weren’t just for decoration.

In medieval times, a heart carved into the wooden shutters meant a girl of marriageable age lived in that house.

No heart usually meant the “listing” was taken or the family wasn’t open to offers 🤫

This is…

📍 Colmar, France

Save this if you’re single and booking flights immediately 😅😂

Long before luxury wellness retreats, Roman emperors escaped to this tiny island for the sea air, sunlight, silence, and dramatic cliffs above the Mediterranean.

the smell of lemons everywhere
warm salty air blowing through narrow alleyways
hidden gardens above the sea

This is…

📍 Capri, Italy

🚤 How to get here
Take a ferry or private transfer from Naples, Sorrento, or the Amalfi Coast. Ferries run frequently but mornings are usually calmer.

What to do
Visit the Blue Grotto
Take the chairlift to Monte Solaro
See the Faraglioni rocks by boat
Walk through Anacapri for a quieter side of the island
Visit Villa Jovis, where Emperor Tiberius once lived

What to eat
Lemon granita
Caprese salad (it originated here)
Fresh seafood pasta
Torta Caprese, the island’s famous chocolate almond cake

📌 Save this for your Italy trip
